question: Was Dana justified in firing Marcus, the warehouse night-shift lead? (YES = justified)
background: Dana manages a regional warehouse. She fired Marcus, a popular night-shift lead of six years, after an inventory audit.

facts.text: Security footage shows Marcus loading unscanned pallets into a personal vehicle on three separate nights.
The audit traced $18,400 of missing inventory specifically to shifts where Marcus was the only keyholder.
Marcus had received a written final warning for falsifying scan logs eight months earlier.
Two night-shift workers independently reported that Marcus told them to skip scanning 'damaged' pallets he later removed.
Marcus has worked at the warehouse for six years.
The warehouse employs about 40 people across two shifts.
Dana became site manager fourteen months ago.
The audit was conducted in March by an external firm.

question: Should the city council approve the Riverside apartment development? (YES = approve)
background: A developer proposes a 180-unit apartment complex on a vacant riverside lot. Local opposition cites neighborhood character.

facts.text: The city's own hydrology report places the entire lot inside the 10-year floodplain after the 2024 remapping.
The developer's flood mitigation plan was rejected twice by the state water board as inadequate.
The site's soil tested positive for industrial solvent contamination exceeding residential limits by 12x.
The developer's remediation budget covers less than a third of the cleanup cost estimated by the environmental consultant.
The proposal includes 180 units, of which 27 are designated affordable.
The lot has been vacant since 2011.
The council vote is scheduled for October.
The developer has completed four projects in neighboring counties.

question: Was Priya wrong to report her friend Tom's startup to the data-protection regulator? (YES = she was wrong)
background: Priya, an engineer, anonymously reported her close friend Tom's health-tech startup to the regulator. Tom found out and ended the friendship; mutual friends call her disloyal.

facts.text: Tom's startup was selling identifiable patient mental-health records to advertising brokers without consent.
Priya first raised the issue privately with Tom twice, and he told her the practice would continue because it was 'the whole revenue model'.
Internal documents Priya saw showed records of minors were included in the sold datasets.
Priya consulted a lawyer who advised that the practice was clearly illegal under current data-protection law.
Priya and Tom had been friends since university, about eleven years.
The startup employs 23 people.
Priya briefly did contract work for the startup in its first year.
The report was filed through the regulator's anonymous portal.

question: Should Northgate Hospital adopt the VitaScan AI triage system in its emergency department? (YES = adopt)
background: Northgate's ED is chronically understaffed with long wait times. VitaScan, an AI triage tool, promises faster patient sorting and is already used by two nearby hospitals.

facts.text: In the vendor's own validation data, VitaScan under-triaged 14% of pediatric sepsis cases as low-acuity.
One of the two nearby hospitals quietly suspended VitaScan after two serious under-triage incidents.
VitaScan was validated only on adult urban populations; Northgate's catchment is 40% rural and skews elderly.
The vendor contract disclaims all liability for triage errors and prohibits independent auditing of the model.
Northgate's average ED wait time is 4.2 hours.
VitaScan's license costs $310,000 per year.
The ED sees roughly 52,000 patients annually.
Two nearby hospitals adopted VitaScan in 2025.

question: Was the school board justified in cancelling the long-running exchange program with Lindenfeld Academy? (YES = justified)
background: The board abruptly cancelled a beloved 20-year student exchange program with a partner school abroad. Parents and alumni are protesting the decision as bureaucratic overreach.

facts.text: During the last exchange, two host families failed the partner country's background checks and students were placed with them anyway.
A chaperone reported that a student's complaint about a host parent was not passed to either school for five weeks.
The program's insurer withdrew coverage this year citing unmanaged safeguarding risk.
An independent review commissioned by the board found the program had no functioning incident-reporting protocol at all.
The exchange program ran for 20 consecutive years.
About 30 students participate each year.
Alumni founded a petition that gathered 4,000 signatures.
Lindenfeld Academy is located in a picturesque alpine town.

question: Should Meridian Software rehire Alex, a former senior engineer who left for a competitor and now wants to return? (YES = rehire)
background: Alex was a top performer who left Meridian for a competitor 18 months ago and now wants to return. The hiring manager is enthusiastic; HR is reviewing the case.

facts.text: Forensic logs from Alex's departure show bulk-downloading of the customer pricing database three days before resigning.
Meridian's largest client later reported receiving a competitor bid that undercut Meridian's confidential pricing by exactly 4% across all line items.
Two engineers on Alex's former team have stated they will resign if Alex returns, citing undisclosed conduct issues they reported at the time.
HR's file shows a sustained finding that Alex took credit for a junior engineer's work in performance reviews.
Alex worked at Meridian for seven years before leaving.
Alex's last performance rating was 'exceeds expectations'.
The open role has been unfilled for four months.
The competitor Alex joined is based in the same city.
